item.text: I’ll inspect the TLA+ module and nearby files, then iterate with `tlapm` and the provided checker until the proof is accepted.

The file is very small: the target is exactly the “no universal set” fact. I’m going to use the standard Russell-subset argument, adding a helper operator above the theorem so the proof has a concrete witness.

(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Any surjection between two finite sets of equal cardinality is          *)
(* an injection.                                                           *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_SurjSameCardinalityImpliesInj ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S), Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T),
         NEW f \in Surjection(S,T)
  PROVE  f \in Injection(S,T)
<1>1. SUFFICES ASSUME NEW a \in S, NEW b \in S, a # b, f[a] = f[b]
      PROVE FALSE
  BY DEF Injection, Surjection, IsInjective
<1>. DEFINE n == Cardinality(S)
<1>. n \in Nat  BY FS_CardinalityType
<1>. PICK g \in Bijection(1..n, S) : TRUE
  BY FS_CardinalityType, Zenon DEF ExistsBijection
<1>2. PICK i,j \in 1 .. n :
          /\ i < j
          /\ \/ g[i] = a /\ g[j] = b
             \/ g[i] = b /\ g[j] = a
  <2>1. PICK i,j \in 1 .. n : i # j /\ g[i] = a /\ g[j] = b
    BY <1>1 DEF Bijection, Surjection
  <2>2. CASE i < j  BY <2>1, <2>2
  <2>3. CASE i > j  BY <2>1, <2>3
  <2>. QED  BY <2>1, <2>2, <2>3
<1>. n-1 \in Nat  BY <1>2
<1>. DEFINE h == [ k \in 1 .. n-1 |-> IF k=j THEN f[g[n]] ELSE f[g[k]] ]
<1>3. h \in Surjection(1..n-1, T)
  <2>1. h \in [1..n-1 -> T]  BY DEF Bijection, Surjection
  <2>2. ASSUME NEW t \in T  PROVE \E k \in 1..n-1 : h[k] = t
    <3>1. PICK s \in S : f[s] = t  BY DEF Surjection
    <3>2. PICK l \in 1..n : g[l] = s  BY DEF Bijection, Surjection
    <3>. HIDE DEF n
    <3>3. CASE l = j
      <4>. /\ i \in 1 .. n-1
           /\ i # j
           /\ h[i] = t
        BY <1>1, <1>2, <3>1, <3>2, <3>3
      <4>. QED  OBVIOUS
    <3>4. CASE l # j /\ l = n
      <4>. /\ j \in 1 .. n-1
           /\ h[j] = t

(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM WFInduction ==
          ASSUME NEW P(_), NEW R, NEW S,
                 IsWellFoundedOn(R, S),
                 \A x \in S : (\A y \in SetLessThan(x, R, S) : P(y))
                    => P(x)
          PROVE  \A x \in S : P(x)
<1> DEFINE T == {x \in S : ~P(x)}
<1>1. SUFFICES ASSUME T # {}
               PROVE  FALSE
  OBVIOUS
<1>2. PICK x \in T : \A y \in T : ~ (<<y, x>> \in R)
  BY <1>1, WFMin, Zenon
<1>3. QED
  BY <1>2 DEF SetLessThan

(***************************************************************************)
(* Theorem WFInductiveDef below justifies recursive definitions based on a *)
(* well-founded ordering.  We first prove it with the hypothesis that the  *)

THEOREM WFMin ==
         ASSUME NEW R, NEW S,
                IsWellFoundedOn(R, S),
                NEW T, T \subseteq S, T # {}
         PROVE  \E x \in T : \A y \in T : ~ (<<y, x>> \in R)
<1> SUFFICES ASSUME \A x \in T : \E y \in T : <<y, x>> \in R
             PROVE  FALSE
  BY Zenon
<1> DEFINE f0 == CHOOSE x \in T : TRUE
           Def(v, n) == CHOOSE x \in T : <<x, v>> \in R
           f[n \in Nat] == IF n = 0 THEN f0 ELSE Def(f[n-1], n)
<1>1. NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def)
  <2>1. NatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, f0, Def)
    BY Zenon DEF NatInductiveDefHypothesis
  <2>. QED
    BY <2>1, NatInductiveDef, Zenon
